problem_summary: 2006 MCM Problem B (discrete): design a cost-efficient airport wheelchair-assistance service for a fictional "Epsilon Airlines" — decide how many wheelchair/escort pairs to deploy and how to schedule/route them to move mobility-impaired passengers between arrival and departure gates so that flights are not delayed and passengers do not miss connections, while minimizing total daily operating cost. This team (#868) frames it as a stochastic, event-driven simulation-optimization problem: build a synthetic-but-realistic day of requests, run a greedy real-time dispatch/scheduling heuristic over a graph model of the airport, and sweep the number of escorts to find the cost-minimizing fleet size per airport.
models_used: Airport as a bidirectional weighted graph: nodes = gates/entrances, edges = corridor travel paths, encoded as an n x n adjacency matrix A of gate-to-gate travel times (distance / 3 mph walking speed)
Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm precomputed all-pairs to build a shortest-travel-time matrix D (delta_xy = shortest time between nodes x and y), used as the metric for all dispatch decisions
Discrete-event / event-driven simulation of one airport day: continuous-time model of passenger arrival/departure and escort movement, advanced by processing a time-sorted request list

Greedy real-time dispatch heuristic with a 4-tier optimality classification (O1-O4): for each request, every escort is graded into the best feasible insertion class (O4 = insert with no schedule shifting; O3 = insert by shifting later tasks but no late departures; O2 = insert by bumping/removing overlapping tasks; O1 = deliver late but within an allowable flight-delay window delta_w), then assign to the best non-empty group, tie-broken by which escort's previous task ends closest to the new arrival gate

Temporal bin-packing formulation of scheduling: each escort's day is a 'bin' and the dispatcher packs as many feasible escort tasks as possible while honoring the deadline constraint t_f = t_d - delta_ad (must reach passenger by departure time minus transfer time)

Cost / objective function: C = Cost_fixed + Cost_miss * X + Cost_delayed * Y, where X = missed flights, Y = total flight-delay hours; Cost_fixed = (10 + 130/(365*24)) * H * N_E combining $10/hr escort wages and $130/yr/chair maintenance-storage over simulation period H

Monte Carlo / stochastic input generation: randomized daily flight schedule (departures evenly spaced 6AM-10PM then perturbed by <1hr random shift), randomized request list (request percentage 0.6% or 1.2% of passengers; mix of call-ahead requests at t=0 and walk-up requests 0.5-5hr ahead), 10 trials per data point with one-sigma error bars
Parameter-sweep optimization: brute-force sweep over number of escorts N_E to locate the cost-minimizing fleet size for each airport/traffic scenario (outer optimization loop wrapping the simulation)
key_techniques: Simulation-driven optimization: nest the discrete-event simulator inside an outer sweep over the decision variable (N_E), treating the simulator as a black-box objective evaluator — a clean way to optimize a system with no closed-form cost

Real, named source data plugged into the cost model: $1018/hr delayed-flight cost (a 1986 figure inflation-adjusted via a cited inflation calculator), $500/missed-flight reimbursement, $10/hr wages, $130/yr/chair maintenance — every coefficient is cited, not invented

Ablation study to justify model complexity: progressively disabling optimality groups O3, O2, O1 (running O4-only, then O4+O3, etc.) to quantify how much each scheduling feature actually saves — showed adding O3 alone saves 5-6 employees / ~$1000/day, proving the adaptive scheduler earns its complexity
Building real airport graphs from Google Maps / satellite imagery (O'Hare T2/T3, LaGuardia, DFW) rather than a toy network, giving the geometry-sensitivity analysis credibility

Deadline-driven feasibility test as the core scheduling primitive: t_e + delta_ea < t_f (escort finishes prior task and reaches arrival gate before the required final time), with a relaxed variant t_e + delta_ea < t_f + delta_w for the late-but-acceptable tier

Auxiliary diagnostic metric — 'schedule density' (fraction of escort time actually spent escorting vs. dead-heading between tasks) — used to interpret why marginal escorts help more in multi-concourse airports
Clean algorithm specification: pseudocode for MainSimulation, OptimalityCheck, and ExecuteRequest with an explicit N_E x 4 optimality matrix O, making the heuristic reproducible
Three-axis sensitivity analysis: across traffic levels, across number of concourses (1/2/4), and across distinct airport layouts (LGA/ORD/DFW), each repeated over traffic scenarios

Scenario-based forecasting: separating a 10% rise in total passengers (which adds proportional flights) from a 10% rise in requests-per-capita (aging population, no new flights), and showing the latter is costlier per request — a non-obvious, decision-relevant insight
why_it_won: "From a judge's seat, this paper wins because it converts a vague operations prompt into a fully instrumented, reproducible decision tool and then USES it to answer the real management questions (how many chairs, how to deploy, what about an aging population). The greedy O1-O4 optimality-tier dispatcher is genuinely clever yet simple to state, and crucially the team proves it is worth its complexity via an ablation that disables tiers one at a time and reports the dollar savings of each — that is exactly the kind of self-skeptical, quantitative justification judges reward. The cost model is anchored in cited, inflation-adjusted real numbers rather than guesses, so the optimal-escort counts ($961-$3846/day for various airports, ≤16 escorts even for the busiest 4-concourse case) feel trustworthy. The team generalized across three orthogonal dimensions (traffic, concourse count, real airport layouts from satellite imagery) and even produced a counterintuitive, policy-relevant finding (per-capita demand growth costs more than equivalent passenger-volume growth) for the aging-population sub-question. It is concrete, validated, honest about weaknesses, and directly actionable for the client — the hallmarks of an Outstanding discrete-problem entry."
section_highlights: "Strong, problem-framing Introduction that immediately identifies the two governing decisions (how many wheelchairs, how to deploy them) and the over/under-provisioning tradeoff — gives the whole paper a clear spine. Methods are partitioned into three crisp phases (pre-simulation input generation, simulation rules/dynamics, optimization), which mirrors how the system actually runs and makes the long methods section easy to follow. The scheduling logic is built up pedagogically from the least-optimal tier (O1, late-but-within-window) to the most-optimal (O4, no reshuffle), each with its own one-line inequality constraint, then consolidated into clean labeled pseudocode (Algorithm 2.1-2.3) and an N_E x 4 optimality matrix — highly reproducible. Results section is disciplined: an explicit Test Protocol (10 trials/point, one-sigma error bars, half of requests known ahead) precedes any plot, then results are organized as a deliberate sensitivity ladder (scheduling-method ablation, across concourses, across airports, aging population). Figures pair real satellite images with their derived node-edge graphs and cost-vs-escort curves. Conclusion is candid about limitations (e.g., the dispatcher can sacrifice several queued passengers to save one long-haul request) before arguing the demonstrated robustness outweighs them, and closes by tying everything back to the client 'Epsilon Airlines.'"
pitfalls_or_limitations: Greedy myopic dispatcher is not globally optimal and the team admits it: a single long-travel request can bump 3-4 short queued tasks, causing several passengers to miss flights to save one — the heuristic optimizes per-request insertion, not total damage; their 'fix' (just add more escorts) sidesteps rather than solves it

Validation is entirely against self-generated synthetic data — there is no real wheelchair-demand or dispatch-log dataset; the request list, flight schedule, and even the demand percentages (0.6%/1.2%) are assumptions, so 'our algorithm works' is shown only against the team's own simulator

Idealized operating assumptions weaken realism: no flight arrival/departure delays from any other cause, escort-to-airplane (jetbridge) walk explicitly ignored, escorts never break/rotate (constant N_E all day with instantaneous shift handoff), wheelchair malfunctions folded into a flat maintenance cost

The 'across airports' conclusion that performance is statistically equivalent for LGA/ORD/DFW arguably undercuts their own motivating premise that geometry matters — it may reflect that they only tested 2-concourse slices at matched traffic, masking the very layout effects (e.g., DFW's ~1-mile circular walk) they hypothesized

Cost coefficients are shaky in places: the delayed-flight cost is a 1986 number merely inflation-adjusted, and the wheelchair maintenance figure is sourced from an amputee life-care-planning page — defensible but thinly grounded for a decision affecting fleet sizing

Scalability/complexity of the dispatcher is never analyzed; rescheduling and bump-and-reinsert on every request could be costly for very large airports, and some test cases were limited to a single concourse explicitly 'due to project time constraints'

Several embedded figure data tables in this markdown extraction look like OCR placeholders (e.g., the perfect y=x line chart, generic 'Node 1..30' graphs), so the quantitative figure values should be treated cautiously when mining this copy
reusable_patterns: Graph + Dijkstra preprocessing as a reusable spatial backbone: model any facility/transport network as a weighted graph, precompute an all-pairs shortest-time matrix D once, then let every downstream decision query D in O(1) — applies to logistics, evacuation, delivery, hospital-portering problems

Tiered greedy assignment with explicit feasibility inequalities: rank candidate insertions into named optimality classes (best -> worst), assign to the best non-empty class with a geometric tie-break (closest prior task) — a clean, explainable alternative to opaque optimization for real-time scheduling/dispatch problems

Simulation-as-objective + outer parameter sweep: when the cost has no closed form, wrap a discrete-event simulator in a loop over the decision variable to locate the optimum; report each point as a mean over N stochastic trials with error bars

Ablation to justify complexity: disable model features one at a time and report the dollar/performance cost of each — turns 'our model is sophisticated' into 'feature X is worth $Y/day,' which directly answers the judge's 'why so complicated?' question

Real-data-grounded cost function with cited, inflation-adjusted coefficients and a clean fixed+variable decomposition (C = Cost_fixed + Cost_miss*X + Cost_delayed*Y) — a transferable template for any cost-minimization MCM problem

Synthetic-but-defensible scenario generator: build inputs from real aggregate facts (passenger volume, ~125 pax/plane, 6AM-10PM activity window, % needing assistance) then add controlled randomness (perturb schedule, mix call-ahead vs walk-up) so the method is tested against many days, not one

Multi-axis sensitivity ladder as the results skeleton: sweep the model across orthogonal drivers (load x topology x real instances), each replicated stochastically, to demonstrate robustness rather than a single point answer

Scenario decomposition for forecasting questions: when asked about a future trend (aging population), split it into distinct mechanisms (more total passengers vs more requests-per-capita) and show they are NOT equivalent — produces a sharp, decision-relevant recommendation
Building model geometry from public imagery (Google Maps / satellite) to make a toy graph into a credible real-instance model — cheap way to add external validity
